title: Initial change to systemd to manage volume mounts
body:
This commit is the initial change to manage volumemounts via systemd and ignition instead of through SSH commands as part of `podman machine start`.

This attempts to fix #15976.

Kirk's TODO:

- [x] Add More Unit Tests where applicable
- [ ] Work with maintainers to decide functionality for existing SSH-mounted userdirs (how do we migrate existing users?)
- [ ] Add comments where applicable in the new functions to clarify why we're adding two systemd files.

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

This introduces a behavioral change in order to manage volume mounts on `podman machine` via systemd files via ignition config, which will persist user-defined mounts through the podman-machine updates and reboots without having to explicitly stop/start.

```release-note
TBD
```

comments.body: does this make it more difficult to be dynamic with mounts?
@baude It definitely makes it a little more awkward for dynamic mounts. It might still be possible to do, but I'd need to think about it for a little because there might be chicken-and-egg problems.

@baude I'm not sure I completely understand the question, but my intent was to make this entirely dynamic based on the content of what's being populated in the `ign.Mounts` struct, so if support for more types of volumes or something is added later it can be easily modified to support those.

@ashley-cui - Can you point me to something to clarify what is meant by "Dynamic Mounts" because I think I'm missing something about what that would be.

@iamkirkbater We're currently looking at adding support for adding/removing volumes to a machine after the machine has been created. Since ignition is only used once - on the creation of the machine, this migration to systemd to manage volume mounts will need some more design considerations for future dynamic support.

Ahh, I see what you mean now.  That makes sense.  Could always just use `systemd` as a source of truth in the volume mounts as well, since the naming is deterministic for both the mount point with systemd - it would always be the path to the mount point.  I'm not sure how the tagging bits work re: vol0, vol1, ... volN, but I could also change the systemd file that creates the mountpoint itself to use the same name as the `.mount` unit, which would then be unique to each mount point.

More than happy to work with y'all to find an appropriate solution :)

Current status - This seems to work only intermittently.  Sometimes the mounts don't come up correctly, sometimes the mountpoint service isn't run. 

More debugging is needed, journalctl and demsg don't seem to have _any_ logs related to these new services even though they work on the machine.

If they're manually run they end up working permanently via `systemctl start` but who _actually_ wants to do that as part of trying to start, since the whole point of doing it this way is to _improve_ the user experience by being more hands-off.

Hopefully I'll have some more time later this week to poke at this again.
A friendly reminder that this PR had no activity for 30 days.
@ashley-cui @baude I think we should move forward on this, WDYT?

@iamkirkbater: PR needs rebase.

A friendly reminder that this PR had no activity for 30 days.
@ashley-cui What is going on with this?

Same status, we paused looking at this since @baude and I were doing research on adding support for dynamic mounts, but it turned out that the currently qemu won't allow us to do what we want it to. Changing to systemd would be beneficial ATM, before possibly moving to apple virt but there are definitely other things to consider that I don't remember off the top of my head.
A friendly reminder that this PR had no activity for 30 days.
With podman 5, i believe this is good to go now (virtio).  Please re-open if you feel otherwise.
